prompt: I have panel data from 50 stores across 2 years (monthly observations). In January 2024, 25 stores adopted a new checkout system while 25 didn't. I want to estimate the causal effect of the new checkout system on monthly revenue. The data has columns: store_id, month, revenue, group (1=treatment, 0=control), post_treatment (1 if after Jan 2024, 0 otherwise). Help me build a rigorous causal analysis.
assertions: Proposes a DAG or explicitly states causal assumptions before estimation
Asks the user to confirm the causal graph or assumptions before proceeding
Identifies this as a Difference-in-Differences design (not just a regression)
States the parallel trends assumption explicitly and explains what it means
Asks the user whether the parallel trends assumption is defensible
Uses CausalPy's DifferenceInDifferences class (not manual PyMC regression)
Uses a PyMC-backed model (cp.pymc_models.*), not sklearn
Runs at least one refutation or robustness check (placebo time, parallel trends test, or DoWhy refuter)
Reports the effect estimate with a credible interval (HDI), not just a point estimate
Uses causal language appropriately (says 'the checkout system caused/increased revenue by X' only if refutation passes)
Produces or offers to produce a report with assumptions, results, and limitations sections
States limitations or threats to validity (e.g., what could violate parallel trends)
